description: A two-step prediction interactive tool for NCERT Grade 6 Science, Chapter 6 (Materials Around Us), Section 6.3.4 — Solubility. Framed around the shikanji (lemonade) story from the textbook. Screen 1 shows sugar crystals about to be stirred into water; Screen 2 shows the clear stirred glass. Students predict the outcome at each step. Correct predictions lock with a one-line dispersion reason; wrong predictions return with a gentle hint. The finish card carries the core concept: 'Sugar is dispersed, not destroyed.' Fully responsive — single-column on mobile, two-column scene+options on tablet and above.

chapter.subject: Science
chapter.section: 6.3.4 What is soluble in water; what is not?
chapter.activity: Activity 6.7 — Let us explore (Sugar in Water)
chapter.story_hook: Ghulan's shikanji (lemonade) — sugar and salt stirred into water

width.description: Maximum container width in pixels. Fully responsive below this.

themeColor.description: Primary accent colour. Drives header gradient and primary buttons.

additionalProps.description: Tool-specific configuration. All fields optional — Activity 6.7 shikanji defaults are used.

steps.description: Override the two default prediction screens. Each step is a full question config.

sceneEmoji.description: Large emoji for the card header, e.g. '🥄'

sceneTitle.description: Short scene title shown in card header.

sceneDesc.description: One or two sentence scene description.

questionText.description: The prediction question the student must answer.

animationClass.description: CSS animation for card entrance. Alternate Left/Right for visual rhythm.

options.description: Prediction choices. Exactly one must have isCorrect: true.

id.description: Unique option id within this step.

label.description: Button label shown to student. May include emoji prefix.

isCorrect.description: Whether this is the correct prediction.

lockReason.description: One-line scientific reason shown when locked. Anchor in dispersion/solubility. Empty string for wrong options.

hintText.description: Gentle contextual hint for wrong taps. References 'sugar-is-not-gone' idea. Empty string for correct option.

finishTitle.description: Headline shown in the completion card header.

finishBody.default: When sugar dissolves in water, it breaks into particles too small to see — but they are still there. That's why the shikanji tastes sweet even though the crystals have 'disappeared'. Dissolving is a physical change — nothing is created or lost.
finishBody.description: Explanation paragraph shown in the completion card body.

showTimer.description: Show elapsed time chip in header and in the completion stats row.

shuffleOptions.description: Reserved. When true, option order would be randomised per attempt.

value.finishBody: Sugar dissolves — its particles spread invisibly through water. It never truly disappears.

examples.description: Extended — add a third step for salt in water

steps.sceneTitle: Spoonful of sugar, glass of water
steps.sceneDesc: Ghulan's mother stirs a heaped spoon of sugar into a glass of water.
steps.questionText: What will happen to the sugar crystals after stirring?

options.label: 🫧 Sugar will disappear
options.lockReason: Sugar dissolves — breaks into invisible particles that spread through water.
options.hintText: Think about sugar in tea — do you see the crystals once they dissolve?

options.label: 🔮 Sugar will stay as crystals
options.hintText: Water is a great solvent. Sugar crystals break apart when stirred.

steps.sceneTitle: After stirring — the glass looks clear
steps.sceneDesc: The water looks completely clear. Ghulan picks up the glass to drink.
steps.questionText: Without looking, how will the water taste?

options.label: 🍬 Tastes sweet
options.lockReason: Sugar is dispersed, not destroyed. The particles are invisible but still present — sweetness remains!
options.hintText: Dissolved sugar is still IN the water — just too small to see.

options.label: 💧 Tastes plain like water
options.hintText: Dissolving hides sugar from your eyes but NOT from your tongue!

value.finishBody: Dissolving is a physical change. Sugar particles spread invisibly through water but remain present — that's why the drink tastes sweet.

defaultSteps.sceneTitle: Spoonful of sugar, glass of water
defaultSteps.sceneDesc: Ghulan's mother stirs a heaped spoon of sugar into a glass of water for shikanji.
defaultSteps.questionText: What will happen to the sugar crystals after stirring?

options.label: 🫧 Sugar will disappear
options.lockReason: Sugar dissolves — it breaks into tiny invisible particles that spread through the water. It's gone from sight, but still there!
options.hintText: Think about what happens when you stir salt into soup — does it vanish, or does it hide?

options.label: 🔮 Sugar will stay as crystals
options.hintText: Water is a great solvent. Try imagining what happens to sugar in tea — do you ever see the crystals at the bottom?

defaultSteps.sceneTitle: After stirring — the glass looks clear
defaultSteps.sceneDesc: The water looks completely clear now. Ghulan picks up the glass to drink his shikanji.
defaultSteps.questionText: Without looking, how will the water taste?

options.label: 🍬 Tastes sweet
options.lockReason: Sugar is dispersed, not destroyed. The particles are invisible but they're still in the water — so the sweetness remains!
options.hintText: If sugar vanished completely, there would be nothing sweet left. But dissolving is not the same as disappearing forever.

options.label: 💧 Tastes plain like water
options.hintText: Dissolving hides sugar from our eyes — but not from our tongue! The particles are still floating in the water.

interactions.desktop: Click option buttons. Correct locks with green reason. Wrong shakes + amber hint returns after 2.8s. 'Next' / 'Finish' button advances.
interactions.mobile: Tap option buttons. Same lock/shake logic. Responsive single-column layout below 600px.
interactions.correctLock: Option turns green with check icon + one-line reason anchored in dispersion. 'Next' / 'Finish' button appears.
interactions.wrongBounce: Option shakes red, amber hint banner appears below options, resets to predict phase after 2.8s.
interactions.completion: Confetti, trophy banner, 3-stat card row, finish concept card with key concept pills and Remember box, reset button.
interactions.reset: Returns to step 1 in predict phase, clears all scores, restarts timer.

responsive.mobile: Single-column layout, smaller fonts, scene stacks above options
responsive.tabletPlus: Two-column layout: 220px scene panel left, options right

agentNotes.whenToUse: Use when a Grade 6 student is studying section 6.3.4 (solubility) or Activity 6.7. Also use as a quick conceptual hook when a student asks 'where does sugar go when it dissolves?' or 'why does the water taste sweet after sugar disappears?'
coreConceptsReinforced: Solubility — sugar is soluble, sand is insoluble
Dissolving is a physical change — no substance is created or destroyed
Dispersion — particles spread too small to see but are still present
Observable evidence — taste persists even when crystals vanish
agentNotes.instructionLine: Pick your prediction for each screen. See if stirring matches what you thought.
